content: It was a Pink Country, indeed.
Half a mile distant was a large City, its pink tintings glistening bravely in the pink sunshine, while hundreds of pink banners floated from its numerous domes.
It was the parrot that aroused them.
it screamed;
They all had short necks and legs, pink hair and eyes, rosy cheeks and pink complexions, and their faces were good-natured and jolly in expression.

They wore rings and necklaces and bracelets and brooches of rose gold set with pink gems, and all four of the new arrivals, both men and women, carried sharp pointed sticks, made of rosewood, for weapons.

"There is some truth in what she says," remarked one of the men, thoughtfully.
Cap'n Bill looked cross for a minute, for he did not like to be called a "gigantic monster," although he realized he was much larger than the pink people.
"That is a country we have never heard of.
Where is it located?"
"It isn't just one country, but a good many countries."
"We have three countries in Sky Island," returned the woman.
"Stole it!
"And they made us slaves," said Trot.
"So we ran away and passed through the Fog Bank and came here," said Button Bright.
Then one of the women came forward and addressed the strangers.
"Your story is the strangest we have ever heard," said she; "and your presence here is still more strange and astonishing.
"Who is Tourmaline?" inquired Trot, doubtfully, for she didn't like the idea of being "taken" to anyone.
"The Queen of the Pinkies.
"Seems to me we've had 'bout enough of kings an' queens," remarked Cap'n Bill.

Until we prove your truth and honor we must regard you as enemies of our race.
"No bird can talk unless inspired by witchcraft."
But this incident had determined the Pinkies to consider our friends prisoners and to take them immediately before their Queen.
"We'll go peaceable," answered Cap'n Bill.
Their clothing was quite dry by this time, although much wrinkled and discolored by the penetrating fog, so at once they prepared to follow the Pinkies.
So the procession moved along the pretty roadways to the City, which they soon reached.
There was a strong high wall of pink marble around it and they passed through a gate made of pink metal bars and found themselves in a most delightful and picturesque town.

It was paved with pink marble and between the street and the houses that lined both sides of it were gardens filled with pink flowers and pink grass lawns, which were shaded by pink trees and shrubbery.
The soldiers had partly recovered their courage and, fearful of the anger of their dreaded Boolooroo, whom the Princesses declared would punish them severely, had ventured to return to the room.
They came rather haltingly, though, and the Captain of the Guards first put his head cautiously through the doorway to see if the coast was clear.
The Captain halted, his soldiers peering curiously over his shoulders and the Six Snubnosed Princesses looking on from behind, where they considered themselves safe.
"Don't come in!
Don't come in!" yelled the Boolooroo in a terrified voice.
The soldiers were secretly glad to observe this, but the Princesses were highly indignant.
"Don't worry," replied Trot.
Cap'n Bill and I perpose runnin' this Island ourselves, after this.
"Can't you see?"
"It looks like it," admitted the Captain.
"Nonsense!" shouted Indigo.
"March in and capture them, Captain!
Never mind if they do slice the Boolooroo.
"I'll rule it!"
Cobalt began to say:
"Well, well!
"Now, you Blue Cap'n, who are you and your soldiers going to obey-me or the snubnosed ones?"
"You!" declared the Captain of the Guards, positively, for he hated the Princesses, as did all the Blueskins.
"So now, Queen Trot, what's next on the program?"
But the umbrella wasn't there, either.
Finally they returned to the great throne room of the palace, where they seated themselves on the throne and tried to think what could possibly have become of the precious umbrella.
While they were sitting and talking together the Captain of the Guards entered and bowed respectfully.
"Well, order 'em out," commanded Trot.
"And, say; get all the soldiers together and tell all the people there's going to be a high time in the Blue City to night.
"The Pinkies!" exclaimed the Captain of the Guards; "why, they're our enemies, your Short Highness."
"Not any more," replied Trot.
Lizzie and Ernestine despised people not made of paper, who had only two or three little boys and girls.
It took a day and a half to make the journey, and the little cousins did not visit each other more than once or twice a year.
So much better for the change!
See, mamma, how round and pink their faces have grown!"
"I wouldn't advise you to depend so much on Colonel Wheeler," Lizzie's mother would sometimes say.
"These military men are rather uncertain characters.
He would never steal anybody else's family!
mrs Adipose, whose name had been suggested by papa, was the fattest of all the dolls.
Captain Allen was a creature who had no real existence.

This Lizzie and Ernestine considered an admirable arrangement; for, as Captain Allen never came home and never wrote, he was as little of an inconvenience to his family as any gentleman can ever hope to be.
Adipose grew a little home sick.
She began to worry about mr Adipose.
"Oh, mamma, it isn't his fault, I am sure it isn't," pleaded Lizzie.
"I have perfect confidence in him.
No, something dreadful has happened,--it's that horrid post office!" and she wrung her hands.
Mamma was very sorry for Lizzie.
Papa wrote to the postmaster, and Ernestine's papa inquired at the Hingham post office, and there was quite a stir over the lost travellers.
Time went on.
Mamma laughed at me, but she doesn't know you as well as I do. Nobody shall ever laugh at you again."
This idea of mine was fostered by the excellent testimonial which I had written myself at the Company's bidding.
However, they insisted.
The doctor began quietly enough.
He asked, as I had anticipated, after the health of my relations.
He wondered if I drank much.
Unfortunately he did not pursue the matter....
"And now," he said, after the hundredth question, "I should like to look at your chest."
He was evidently determined to see my chest.
Nothing could move him from his resolve.
Trembling, I prepared for the encounter.
"This," I said to myself at the third knock, "has torn it.
"For 'eving's sake do something, you fool.
Can't you hear the gentleman?" I suppose that roused it, for at the next knock he passed on to an adjacent spot....
"I don't believe he likes my chest."
Without a word he got out his stethoscope and began to listen to me.
But it was boring for me, because I really had very little to do.
I could have bitten him in the neck with some ease ... or I might have licked his ear.
I moistened my lips and spoke.
"Am I dying?" I asked in a broken voice.
"Don't talk," he said.
"Just breathe naturally."
By and by he went and listened behind my back.
It is very bad form to listen behind a person's back.
I did not tell him so however.
I wanted him to like me.
"Yes," he said.
"Now cough."
"I haven't a cough," I pointed out.
Extremely nervous, I did my celebrated imitation of a man with an irritating cough.
"Yes," said the doctor.
"Go on."
I shall devote more time to mimicry in future.
The doctor came round to where I could see him again.
"Now cough like this," he said.
It went well.
I got an encore.
"I'm not bothering about this stupid insurance business now.
Can you promise me a week?"
He said nothing.
He took my wrists in his hands and pressed them.
It was evident that grief over mastered him and that he was taking a silent farewell of me.
I bowed my head.
However, he wouldn't let me go.
He resisted....
I realised suddenly that I had mistaken his meaning, and that he was simply feeling my pulses.
"There is just one thing more.
Sit down."
"Now cross your knees."
I crossed my knees.
My chest may have disappointed him....
This time he should not be disappointed.
I was taking no risks.
Almost before his hand reached my knee, my foot shot out and took him fairly under the chin.
She sighs at intervals and occasionally lays down her work and presses both hands to her heart.
A sympathetic audience will have no difficulty in guessing that she is in love.
Did you have a pleasant dinner party last night, Jane?
Such an interesting man, my dear.
We found that we agreed upon all the main principles of Art, considered as such.
When I was in Shropshire last week---- What was your man's name?
You know how difficult it is to catch names when one is introduced.
A letter for you, Miss.
In short, dear Miss Prendergast, I ask you to marry me, and I will come in person for my answer.
Bootle!"
mr Bootle!
The man who took me down to dinner!
Well!
Fancy!
How do you do?
Did you get back safely last night?
Yes, james.
A younger sister!
Don't go!
I didn't know your Christian name-I didn't know you had a sister.
But what can we do?
Nothing.
As a man of honour I cannot withdraw.
So two lives are ruined!
Jane must never know.
Good bye!
